splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on when actuarial assumptions must be finalized for calculating employer withdrawal liability under ERISA. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be fixed by the end of the prior plan year, providing employers with greater predictability about potential liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects sentencing at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises questions about judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, extending the court's authority to revoke supervised release beyond the original term.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and the term expires as originally set regardless of the supervisee's flight.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: The circuits split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Congress intended when bringing successive collateral attacks. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are categorically barred from raising any claim they previously presented, in addition to Section 2255(h)'s requirements. If it does not apply, they need only satisfy 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners filing § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s own requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The outcome could reshape how courts evaluate standing for such challenges, affecting rules on mail-in voting, ballot counting deadlines, and election administration practices nationwide.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates suffer a cognizable injury from laws that allegedly dilute lawful votes or change election rules, granting standing to challenge such laws pre-election.

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not suffer a sufficiently concrete and particularized injury from ballot-counting laws to establish Article III standing for pre-election challenges.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations (Auer/Kisor deference) when construing ambiguous provisions of the Sentencing Guidelines that expand criminal liability.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether courts should apply the rule of lenity or defer to the Sentencing Commission's commentary when Guidelines provisions are ambiguous in a way that affects criminal punishment. At least three circuits hold lenity trumps deference, while the Third Circuit has held that deference prevails. This split has deepened after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from using delegated power to expand criminal liability.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference takes precedence over the rule of lenity, allowing deference to anti-defendant readings of ambiguous Guidelines provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: The circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in the safety-valve provision. This determines whether defendants must meet all three criminal-history criteria to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether possessing any one of the criteria is disqualifying. The split leads to disparate sentencing outcomes depending on geography.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or.'

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' commentary (Application Note 1) can validly expand the definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy, when the guideline text does not mention them.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds conspiracy and attempt to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor. Some circuits defer to the commentary and treat conspiracy as a qualifying predicate, while others hold the guideline text is unambiguous in excluding inchoate offenses. This affects career-offender designations and dramatically impacts sentencing ranges.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is a reasonable interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling deference under Stinson, making conspiracy a qualifying 'controlled substance offense' for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that the definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) is unambiguous and does not include inchoate offenses, so Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline's scope and is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), may be considered as an 'extraordinary and compelling reason' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether courts may consider non-retroactive sentencing changes—particularly the First Step Act's elimination of mandatory stacked § 924(c) sentences—when evaluating compassionate release motions. Some circuits hold that such changes can never contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others permit courts to consider them, especially in combination with other factors. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to allow this, but some circuits have held the Commission exceeded its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or in combination with other factors, cannot constitute an 'extraordinary and compelling reason' for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state controls a substance not listed on the federal schedules.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally scheduled. Some circuits hold that state offenses qualify regardless of whether the substance is federally controlled, while others require a match to federal schedules. This affects base offense levels and career-offender designations.

positions.label: State offense qualifies regardless of federal scheduling
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines broadly define 'controlled substance offense' to include offenses under state law, with no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the definition.

positions.label: Substance must be federally scheduled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: The circuits disagree on who gets the benefit of property appreciation that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and its conversion to Chapter 7. The Ninth Circuit held that appreciation belongs to the estate, while other circuits have held that it belongs to the debtor, consistent with the statute's purpose of encouraging good-faith Chapter 13 filings.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the bankruptcy estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), combined with the broad scope of § 541(a), means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure and legislative history establish that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to good-faith converting debtors, not the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees filed in a criminal case.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment—filed within a criminal case but seeking civil-type relief—is governed by the 30-day civil appeal deadline or the 14-day criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Manchester Farming Partnership

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that because a Hyde Amendment motion is filed in the criminal case, the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the terms (but not the fact or duration) of their imprisonment must proceed through habeas corpus or may bring civil rights claims.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoners challenging BOP decisions about the terms of confinement—such as prerelease custody transfers under the First Step Act—or whether civil rights actions (e.g., Bivens) are also available. The circuits have produced irreconcilable decisions on the boundary between habeas and civil rights claims for over half a century.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive for challenges affecting release timing
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner's challenge effectively seeks earlier release from confinement, habeas corpus is the exclusive federal remedy, foreclosing civil rights actions.

positions.label: Civil actions may be available for conditions-of-confinement claims
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to conditions or terms of confinement—as distinct from the fact or duration of confinement—may proceed as civil rights actions rather than through habeas.

circuits.name: Third Circuit
circuits.caseName: Woodall v. Federal Bureau of Prisons

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release at revocation, or whether revocation sanctions must be limited to the purposes of supervised release.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for supervised release violations at revocation, placing it at what dissenting judges describe as the 'extreme' of a circuit split. Other circuits hold that revocation sentences must focus on the breach of trust and the purposes of supervised release rather than retribution for the underlying violation.

positions.label: Courts may punish for violations at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider punishment as a purpose when sentencing a defendant at a supervised release revocation hearing, and may expressly punish for the violation conduct.

positions.label: Revocation sanctions limited to supervised release purposes
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sanctions should be tied to the breach of trust represented by the violation and the statutory purposes of supervised release, rather than retribution for the violation itself.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents referencing private medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine. This creates tension with circuits that have taken a narrower view of the collateral order doctrine's application to sealing orders involving private information.

positions.label: Medical privacy sealing denials are immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal medical records involves a value of high order that is effectively unreviewable on appeal from final judgment, satisfying the collateral order doctrine.

positions.label: Sealing denials generally not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine because the interest can be adequately protected through other means such as post-judgment appeal.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier... with respect to the transportation of property,' extends to preempt state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting or supervising motor carriers. This affects liability exposure across the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are 'related to' the broker's services with respect to the transportation of property and are therefore preempted by the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or supervision of carriers are not preempted because they do not relate to the broker's 'prices, routes, or services.'

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of liability and available remedies under 42 U.S.C. § 1981?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve a split in how circuits evaluate discrimination claims arising from the operation of business establishments. The Second Circuit's approach to the relevant legal questions was found sufficiently important to warrant review, suggesting a circuit split on applicable standards for liability and damages.

positions.label: Broader standard for discrimination claims
positions.summary: Holds that a broader set of legal standards applies to discrimination claims against hospitality or business establishments, allowing more expansive theories of liability and remedies.

positions.label: Narrower standard for discrimination claims
positions.summary: Holds that a more restrictive set of standards governs discrimination claims in the hospitality or business context, limiting available theories and remedies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus grounds — such as alleged errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence — may be considered in compassionate release motions. Some circuits allow such considerations as one factor, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for § 2255 habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider claimed legal errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot use compassionate release for habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for relitigating the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be brought under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
